topics.topic: Diversity in Plants and Animals Around Us

cards.text: The variety of plants and animals found in a particular region contributes to the biodiversity of that region.

Each member in the biodiversity of a region has a different role to play. For example, trees provide food and shelter to some birds and other animals, animals help in spreading seeds after eating fruits, and so on.

Nature Walk Overview

Dr Raghu and Maniram chacha lead a nature walk organized by a science teacher, madam Sulekha, to help students explore and learn about plants and animals.

Dr Raghu's Role

Dr Raghu, a scientist, oversees the walk and encourages students to appreciate the diversity and beauty in nature.

Maniram chacha's Expertise

Known for mimicking bird calls and identifying various plants and animals, Maniram chacha contributes significantly to the nature walk activities.

Students' Preparations
  • Carry a notebook, pen, and water bottle for recording observations.
  • Respect all living creatures by observing them without disturbance.

Observing Nature

The nature walk includes exploring various plants, recognizing birds, and observing the behavior of butterflies and monkeys.

Sensory Observations
  • Notice the variety of smells around the park.
  • Listen to different animal sounds and bird chirps.

Diversity in Nature

Each bird has a unique chirp, symbolizing nature's diversity.

Bird Call Mimicking

Students enjoy copying Maniram chacha as he mimics different bird calls, promoting engagement with nature.

Sharing Observations

Encouragement to share and discuss personal observations of nature with friends and teachers, enhancing learning and reflection.

Activity 2.1: Planning a Nature Walk
  • Organize a walk to nearby natural locations with a teacher.
  • Observe and record weather conditions and wildlife observed.

Collecting Treasures
  • Gather fallen leaves or flowers to create a scrapbook.
  • Ensure plants and trees remain undisturbed by not plucking leaves or flowers.

Recording Observations

Document different features of plants, like stem height, leaf shape, and flower characteristics.

Plant Features
  • Plants may have tall or short, hard or soft stems.
  • Leaves differ in shape and arrangement on stems or branches.
  • Flowers vary in color, shape, and scent.

Activity 2.2: Exploring Biodiversity

Biodiversity is the variety of plants and animals in a region, each playing unique roles.

Ecosystem Interdependence
  • Trees provide food and shelter, while animals help seed dispersal.
  • Recognition that all living things contribute to ecosystem balance.

topics.topic: How to Group Plants and Animals?

cards.text: Some plants grow really tall and have hard, thick, brown, and woody stems. Their branches typically start higher up on the stem and away from the ground. These plants are called trees. For example, a mango tree.

Some plants are not as tall as trees. These plants often have many brown woody stems that start branching very close to the ground. These stems are hard but not as thick as the stem of a tree. These plants are called shrubs. For example, a rose plant is a shrub.
Some plants are typically small with soft and green stems. These are known as herbs. For example, a tomato plant is a herb.
Some plants with weak stems need support to climb and grow, and are called climbers.
Some plants creep along the ground and are called creepers.

In some leaves, you can observe a net-like pattern of veins on both sides of a thick middle vein. This pattern is called reticulate venation. For example, leaves of hibiscus exhibit reticulate venation.
In some leaves, you may observe that the veins run parallel. This pattern is called parallel venation. For example, the leaves of banana plants and grasses exhibit parallel venation.
Plants that have seeds with two cotyledons are called dicotyledons (dicots).
Dicot plants have reticulate venation and a taproot system while monocot plants have parallel venation and a fibrous root system.

Grouping Plants
Plants have different features such as stem thickness, leaf shape, and flower types which can be used for grouping.

Characteristics of Stems
Stems vary in height, thickness, and hardness in plants, providing clues on plant classification.

Categorizing by Height
Plants can be grouped as herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their height and the type of stem they have.

Observational Activity
Consider plants' height and stem texture and examine whether the branches arise close to the ground or higher up the stem.

Trees
Plants with tall, hard, thick, brown woody stems and branches starting higher up the stem are called trees, like mango trees.

Shrubs
Plants with multiple brown woody stems starting close to the ground and not as tall as trees, such as rose plants.

Herbs
Small plants with soft green stems, often more manageable than trees or shrubs. E.g., tomato plants.

Climbers and Creepers
Weak-stemmed plants that need support to climb (climbers) or creep along the ground (creepers).

Leaf Venation
  1. Reticulate Venation: Net-like vein patterns, seen in hibiscus leaves.
  2. Parallel Venation: Parallel-running veins seen in banana leaves.

Root Types
  1. Taproot: A single main root with smaller side roots, seen in mustard plants.
  2. Fibrous Root: Thin, similar-sized roots branching at the stem base, like in grass plants.

Plant Examples
  1. Chickpeas: Taproot and reticulate venation.
  2. Wheat: Fibrous roots and parallel venation.

Seed Cotyledons
  1. Dicots: Seeds with two parts, like chickpea.
  2. Monocots: Seeds with one part, like maize.

Animal Movements
Animals show various movements like flying, running, and jumping, using structures like wings and legs.

Animal Grouping
Animals can be classified based on movement methods, shape, size, and other physical characteristics.

Silent Valley Movement
Focused on preserving forest biodiversity in Kerala, successfully stopping the construction of a hydroelectric dam.

topics.topic: Plants and Animals in Different Surroundings

cards.text: The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.
The place where plants and animals live is called their habitat. For example, the habitat of sea turtles is the sea or the ocean.
Some animals, such as frogs, can live in water as well as on land. These are called amphibians.

Fishes and Goats
  • Fishes live in water with streamlined bodies and use fins for movement.
  • Goats reside in grassy terrains and rely on legs for mobility.

Biodiversity in Regions
  • Plants and animals differ from one region to another.
  • Cactus with thick stems adapts to deserts, deodar trees with sloping branches to cold mountains.
  • Different regions offer different conditions leading to varied biodiversity.

Desert Conditions
  • Hot deserts in Rajasthan have extreme temperatures: warm days and cold nights.
  • Plants in deserts develop water-storing capacities to survive harsh climates.

Mountainous Climate
  • Conical shapes of trees like deodar let snow slide off easily.
  • Trees in cold regions have adaptations for snow and low temperatures.

Camels in Different Deserts
  • Camels in hot deserts have long legs and wide hooves to navigate sand.
  • Camels in cold deserts like Ladakh have shorter legs.
  • Hot desert camels have one hump, cold desert camels have two for food storage.

Camels' Adaptations
  • In desert conditions, camels excrete minimal urine and have dry dung.
  • Their adaptations help them preserve water and survive with scarce resources.

Rhododendrons
  • Rhododendrons in Nilgiris are shorter with smaller leaves to resist strong winds.
  • The plant adapts differently in Sikkim, growing taller to suit its environment.

Whales and Fish
  • Whale and fish sightings in Andaman speak to aquatic biodiversity.
  • Streamlined fish bodies enhance their swimming ability in water.

Adaptations
  • Special adaptations enable plants and animals to survive particular conditions.
  • Fitting examples are the deodar trees and rhododendrons.

Habitats Defined
  • Habitats provide essential resources like food, water, and shelter.
  • Common habitat examples: sea for turtles, deserts for camels, mountains for rhododendrons.

Salim Ali's Contributions
  • Salim Ali, the 'Birdman of India', documented and contributed to the preservation of bird diversity.
  • Notable works include a 10-volume book series on Indian Subcontinent birds.

Terrestrial and Aquatic Habitats
  • Terrestrial habitats: forests, mountains, deserts, grasslands.
  • Aquatic habitats: ponds, lakes, rivers, oceans.

Amphibians
  • Frogs exemplify amphibians that live both on land and water.

Conservation Projects
  • 'Project Tiger' aims to protect Bengal Tigers.
  • The 'Cheetah Reintroduction Project' was launched to restore Cheetah populations.
  • Protected zones for the Great Indian Bustard in Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Maharashtra.

Sacred Groves
  • Sacred groves in India are locally protected forest areas.
  • They host diverse flora and fauna, conserved by local communities.
We are surrounded by a large variety of plants and animals. Such variety of plants and animals is a part of biodiversity.
Plants and animals can be grouped on the basis of similarities and differences among them.
Plants have similarities and differences based on features associated with roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and so on.
The method of arranging things into groups based on their common features is called grouping.
Plants can be grouped into herbs, shrubs, and trees based on their heights, types of stem, and branching patterns.
Plants can also be grouped as dicotyledons (dicots) and monocotyledons (monocots) based on the number of cotyledons in their seeds.
Monocots generally exhibit parallel venation in their leaves and possess fibrous roots while dicots typically exhibit reticulate venation in their leaves and possess taproots.
Animals have different types of movement that can be a basis for their grouping.
Biodiversity of different regions varies because of distinct environmental conditions.
The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.
The place where plants and animals live is their habitat.
Based on their habitats, animals and plants can be grouped into terrestrial and aquatic.
Due to damage of their habitats, plants and animals lose their homes, food and other resources resulting in the loss of biodiversity.
We must protect biodiversity to ensure that our planet is full of life, helping plants and animals to survive and thrive.
